notes:
- Key concepts introduced
  - Multi-agent system / teams: multiple agents can be part of a team, each possibly of different types and using different tools, collaborating on a shared task.
  - Specialized agents (example): plot agent (creates an engaging plot focusing on Pokémon's journey), character agent (develops characters, describes Pokémon and villain including motivation/background), ending agent (works on the ending of the story). Emphasis on specialization over "jack of all trades".
  - Shared context: team members share the same context so later agents see outputs/inputs from earlier agents (e.g., character agent sees the plot created by the plot agent).
  - Team presets in agent chat / Autogen: frameworks provide team configurations/presets (round-robin group chat is a default/example).
  - Round-robin (roundroin / round doming) team: a team configuration where participants take turns in sequence (first -> second -> third, etc.). This is the most common interaction pattern discussed.
  - Looping in round-robin: the sequence can be sent back to the first agent again (i.e., multiple execution turns/iterations).
  - Primary + critique agent pattern: a configuration where a primary agent produces output and a critique agent evaluates it.
  - Max turns (team config attribute): limits the number of times agents are executed (e.g., "max turns: 3" = first execution, second execution, third execution). Important: it's the number of execution turns, not number of "circles" necessarily.
  - Two common use-case interaction patterns:
    - Full-linear workflow: multiple agents each handle parts (product manager writes requirements, backend engineer builds backend, frontend engineer builds frontend) to produce a full product.
    - Targeted single-agent interaction: a specific agent handles a focused question (e.g., only the backend engineer answers a backend question) without routing through others.
  - Autogen/chat completion participants argument: when defining a round-robin team, agents are provided as participants; this is where developers may encounter errors if mishandled.
  - Components of team/agent config (briefly mentioned): component, description, label, type (metadata fields for agents).

- Brief explanations / clarifications
  - Specialization is preferable: one specialist agent focusing on a narrow part yields better results than a single generalist trying to do everything.
  - Shared context saves repeated re-sending: without shared context across agents (e.g., using different LLMs like OpenAI + Claude separately), you must re-provide context between systems, which is cumbersome; frameworks that share context simplify this.

- Analogies or metaphors used
  - Software team analogy: product manager / backend engineer / frontend engineer representing different agent roles in building an application.
  - OpenAI + Claude example: like using two different services where you must manually pass context between them (demonstrates problem without shared-context framework).
  - "Jack of all trade" metaphor: one agent trying to do everything is compared to a jack of all trades, implying they'll do each task poorly.

- Any code, commands, or config shown (explicit or implied)
  - Team config notions (implied config fields/arguments):
    - participants: list of agent participants passed to chat completion / team constructor.
    - max_turns: integer (example value: 3) meaning number of times agents will run.
    - team type / preset: "round robin group chat" (label/name).
    - agent metadata fields referenced: component, description, label, type.
  - Phrases suggesting API/usage:
    - "in the team you can provide participants as the argument"
    - "your autofill. okay. so your chat completion it is going to give agents here many time"
  - No literal code snippet was provided.

- Warnings, gotchas, or important caveats
  - Participants argument can cause errors: when providing many agents to chat completion as participants, developers may hit errors—"participants is the problem... you will face that error believe me."
  - Max turns semantics: max_turns counts agent executions (first, second, third), not necessarily full cycles; understand this to avoid misconfiguring expected iterations.
  - Without a shared-context framework, using multiple LLMs requires manually re-supplying context between them (tedious and error-prone).
  - "Jack of all trades" caveat: a single agent trying to do everything may not perform tasks properly compared to specialized agents.
  - UI/diagram frustrations: minor note — drawing arrows/diagramming team flow can be error-prone ("arrows are always a problem here").

- Comparisons or lists (potential table candidates)
  - Team interaction styles (listable/comparable):
    - Round-robin team: participants take turns sequentially; shared context; can loop back; common/default preset.
    - Primary + critique setup: primary generates output, critique agent evaluates/responds.
    - Full-linear vs single-agent target workflows (described in "two most common use cases").
  - Agent role examples (could be tabulated):
    - Plot agent: creates plot (Pokemon journey focus).
    - Character agent: develops characters, backgrounds, motivations.
    - Ending agent: crafts story ending; relies on context from other agents.
  - Team config fields to consider:
    - participants, max_turns, component, description, label, type.

- Direct quotes worth preserving
  - "So that is the whole idea everyone of using teams and again multi- aent system we can have they each can be of different type they can be of different they can be using different different tools as well right"
  - "You cannot have someone who is a jack of all trade doing everything properly."
  - "In a team the context is shared and all of them like this will know that okay character agent it will know that yes this is the plot which I has been given to me."
  - "Round robin group chat. A team that runs on a group chart with participant taking turns in roundroin fashion."
  - "Round doming group chat is a simple yet effective team configuration where all agents share the same context."
  - "Let's say you are using open AI and you are using claude. So you asked OpenAI to write some code then you go to claude and maybe you are testing the code out here. So you will have to give the context again."
  - "Once I have defined this particular team, I can just have this team and see what exactly in here."
  - "What this max turns three means here everyone is that this particular team it is going to take maximum number of three turns not three circle... max turns means number of time it agents will kind of executed. First execution, second execution, third execution."
